perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the law of the State of California without regard to the conflicts of law provisions thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Company will not solicit or seek to employ any key employee or executive officer of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had contact or became aware of in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the solicitation or employment of any such person (i) resulting from general advertisements for employment conducted by the Company (including any recruitment efforts conducted by any recruitment agency, provided that the Company has not directed such recruitment efforts at such person), (ii) if such person approaches the Company on an unsolicited basis or (iii) following the cessation of such person’s employment with Actuate without any solicitation or encouragement by the Company. This Agreement shall be governed by the law of the State of California without regard to the conflicts of law provisions thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the choice of law clause to be within the non-solicitation clause reduces its prominence and could lead to enforceability issues, as it is not clearly separated as a governing term of the entire agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1856 states that a written agreement is presumed to contain all the terms of the agreement. By embedding the choice of law clause within a specific clause about non-solicitation, it could be argued that it only applies to that clause and not the entire agreement, creating ambiguity.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The changed text embeds the choice of law clause within the non-solicitation clause, which, according to the Law Explanation, could imply it only applies to that specific clause and not the entire agreement. The Law Explanation states that CCP § 1856 presumes a written agreement contains all the terms of the agreement. By possibly limiting the choice of law clause to the non-solicitation clause, the changed text creates ambiguity regarding whether the choice of law applies to the entire agreement. This ambiguity creates a structural flaw that affects the clarity and enforceability of the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides the text of CCP 1856, however, it does not specify that burying a term like this is inherently contradictory. However, the LLM explanation is that it creates ambiguity, which falls under the legal contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: The prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.changed_text: During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Company will not solicit or seek to employ any key employee or executive officer of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had contact or became aware of in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the solicitation or employment of any such person (i) resulting from general advertisements for employment conducted by the Company (including any recruitment efforts conducted by any recruitment agency, provided that the Company has not directed such recruitment efforts at such person), (ii) if such person approaches the Company on an unsolicited basis or (iii) following the cessation of such person’s employment with Actuate without any solicitation or encouragement by the Company. The prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.explanation: The attorneys' fees clause, when relocated to the non-solicitation section, becomes less clear in its application to the entire contract. This undermines the intended coverage of the clause, potentially limiting its enforcement to only disputes arising from the non-solicitation section.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 1717 allows for the recovery of attorneys' fees in contract actions where the contract provides for it. By placing this clause within a specific section, its general applicability to the entire contract is weakened, potentially preventing recovery of fees for breaches outside of the non-solicitation context. This is especially true since this section describes specific exemptions to the nonsolicitation clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of attorney’s f
perturbation.justification: The relocation of the attorneys' fees clause creates a structural flaw that undermines its clarity and enforceability, potentially contradicting Cal. Civ. Code § 1717. The law snippet states that attorney's fees apply to the entire contract unless specifically negotiated otherwise. By embedding the clause within the non-solicitation section, the changed text introduces ambiguity regarding its applicability to other contract sections, potentially limiting its scope. This placement could be interpreted as restricting the clause's effect only to disputes arising from the non-solicitation provision, contradicting the intent for it to apply contract-wide, as suggested by the law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement shall be held by a court or other tribunal of competent jurisdiction to be illegal, invalid or unenforceable, such provisions shall be limited or eliminated to the minimum extent necessary so that this Agreement shall otherwise remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Company will not solicit or seek to employ any key employee or executive officer of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had contact or became aware of in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the solicitation or employment of any such person (i) resulting from general advertisements for employment conducted by the Company (including any recruitment efforts conducted by any recruitment agency, provided that the Company has not directed such recruitment efforts at such person), (ii) if such person approaches the Company on an unsolicited basis or (iii) following the cessation of such person’s employment with Actuate without any solicitation or encouragement by the Company. In the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement shall be held by a court or other tribunal of competent jurisdiction to be illegal, invalid or unenforceable, such provisions shall be limited or eliminated to the minimum extent necessary so that this Agreement shall otherwise remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.explanation: The severability clause is moved to the section on Non-Solicitation. This makes it seem as though the severability applies only to that specific clause, instead of the agreement as a whole. It's now within the bounds of the nonsolicitation clause, which hurts the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 1599 discusses the impact of illegality of part of a contract. If a contract has several distinct objects, of which one at least is lawful, and one at least is unlawful, in whole or in part, the contract is valid as to the lawful and void as to the unlawful. By moving the severability clause within the nonsolicitation section, one could argue that it only applies to that section. If another section of the agreement is deemed unlawful by a court, it may not be severable since the explicit severability clause is only found in section 7.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1599 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF A CONTRACT [1549 - 1615] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 4. Object of a Contract [1595 - 1599] ( Chapter 4 enacted 1872. ) 1599. Where a contract has several distinct objects, of which one at least is lawful, and one at least is unlawful, in whole or in part, the contract is void as to the latter and valid as to the rest. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw of moving the severability clause creates a legal contradiction because it limits the clause's application to only the non-solicitation section. Cal. Civ. Code § 1599 states that if a contract has distinct objects, some lawful and some unlawful, the contract is valid as to the lawful and void as to the unlawful. By restricting the severability clause, if another part of the contract besides the non-solicitation agreement is deemed unlawful, it may not be severable. Therefore, the changed text undermines the severability of the entire agreement, creating a contradiction as the original text's intent was for the severability clause to apply to the entire contract, not just one section.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement supersedes all prior discussions and writings and constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Further, nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as prohibiting or restricting either party from independently developing, acquiring, and marketing products, services, and other materials, which are similar to or competitive In any geographic area and in any form with the other party’s product(s) or service(s). However, this paragraph shall not be deemed to grant to either party any license under the other party’s copyrights or patents. This Agreement supersedes all prior discussions and writings and constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By placing the entire agreement clause within the section describing the limitations of the agreement regarding independent development and competition, one can argue it only applies to that area, instead of applying to the entirety of the contract. This will make it more difficult to include prior agreements and understandings.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1856 describes the Parol Evidence Rule. The rule states that a written agreement is presumed to contain all the terms of the agreement. By placing the clause as part of the independent development clause, it creates ambiguity whether prior agreements related to other aspects of the business relationship, outside of independent development, are superseded.

(a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by embedding the 'entire agreement' clause within a section concerning independent development. This undermines the clause's intended effect of superseding all prior agreements related to the contract's subject matter. CCP § 1856(a) states that 'Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.' By limiting the scope of the 'entire agreement' clause to the section on independent development, the modified text creates ambiguity and potentially allows for prior agreements on other matters to be considered, directly contradicting the purpose of the clause and the statute. The placement makes it more difficult to claim prior agreements are invalid, creating a structural flaw that results in a legal contradiction.
